speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape two. October 22, 1996. The survivor is Leo Zapiler. Interviewer is Toni Katsh Binstock, Denver, Colorado, United States of America, and the interview is being done in English. Mr. Zapiler, when we finished the last tape, you were telling about your journey when you got to Lutsk. Tell about how you were able to take care of your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, on the road we go out in the field, on the land, and grab potatoes, beans, chicken. My wife didn't want to eat because I couldn't kill the chicken. But I had the guy what [LAUGHS] killed them. And she didn't want to each because the guy killed not kosher. OK, we cooked and we feed everybody. Now, I came in Chemnitz, and I came in in a bakery. And he want to give me only one bread and I need so many people to feed. I-- I took-- I took something. I says, if you not going to let me take the bread, I kill you right here. So he was afraid from me. And I went and took all the bread out from the back, from the hallway and brought the bread and give it to everybody to eat. And we stayed in the forest everyday. We couldn't go further. The nighttime we would go further. And we went to a town, Wlodawa, and nobody there was nothing. They didn't know it's a war.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't know it was a war?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't know it. So, well, I had a horse and wagon. I left the horse on the field, and I went with the train. Took my family and went to Kovel, to Lutsk. And in Lutsk they didn't know it's a war either. So I went to a guy, a chauffeur. And his truck, he had a brand new truck that was shipped from Kiev. And we go. He want to make a road Kiev-Chelm. So we're going to make [INAUDIBLE]. And I worked for him until May.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who all in your family was with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family, we took an apartment

speaker: interviewer
text: And were your brother and sister still with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not my brother and not my sister, just my family. My sister was with me in Brest Litovsk. And my brother went back to Warsaw. So she says if the brother went back, she goes back, too. And she will come. She will pick up some clothes and come back but she never come back. She lived with my brother in Warsaw. He had a factory for buggies for kids. Buggies for kids. And she lived with him. And her husband was with me. So he says, she went back, I go back. He went back, too. When the Bug this River Bug was frozen so they can walk over even back then in wintertime.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the winter of '39, '40?

speaker: interviewee
text: '40, Yeah, 1940. And I says I'm not going back because it's cold, and I wouldn't have with what to eat. I'll stay here until summer. Until summer, I couldn't go back. Then I was working for that guy, and one day, the Russian picks up all of them and put them in the train. They didn't took me, but I told my wife, go in the garage. Sit in the garage because this night they going to pick up everybody. My wife's sister was with her and her husband. The husband told my wife, let's go be with everybody. Let's go with us. We're not going in the garage. We stay here. OK, I was going with two soldiers and picked up the people what didn't belong in Lutsk and put them in the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were these Jews, only Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just was Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no other ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where they were going on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yeah, I'll tell you where we go. So I told my wife to stay in the garage. And I was to pick up the people, bring to the train and go again and go again. I came in the morning, 6 o'clock in the morning and Maria is in the train and hollered, Da, Da, Da, Da.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maria, your daughter, was in the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my wife was already, [LAUGHS] they picked up my wife, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your son?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not me but five, six trucks. They picked up my wife with the kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: And David, too, your son David?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I came there, and Maria hollered, Da, Da, Da, Da. So I called that guy, that officer, and told him, I'm going, too in that one. He says, you not going. You stay with me. I says, my wife is in there in the train. I'm going to stay with you, take it out, my wife with the kids and then I'll stay.

speaker: interviewer
text: He says, I can't do that. I can't. How you go? No, write, and they should send her back. I can write to them where they're going to take her. They should send her back.

speaker: interviewee
text: I says, then you send for me, too. I go there. I go in the train. And I went back, and I brought bread and whiskey. And I took some clothes, and I went right back to the train. And I went in the train. And they took us, was four weeks on the train to Siberia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your wife's sisters with you then, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my wife's sister and her husband, my wife. And a lot of people, a full train. It was four weeks until Siberia. And in Siberia, we started working again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you? Do you know the name in Siberia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until the war broke out. When the war with the Germans broke out, they released us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me go back a little bit. Let's go back to in Siberia when you were there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the place that you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was it-- no it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me about staying there in Siberia, what it was like.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Siberia we worked in forest.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you all live together, or were the men and women separated?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, families together.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of place did you live in?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was like a, they built like a shed, like a hotel, rooms. We had our own room. My sister-in-law with her husband, had their own room. I had the own room. We was four, we had a bigger room. And we worked in the forest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know that you were-- I mean, did they tell you what was happening? Did they offer you Soviet citizenship or anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: They just took you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, just took you. And we went there until the war broke out, Germans with the Russians. Then they came and the SS, you already free. Go wherever you want.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, let me go back and understand. In Siberia where you were, was that like a camp? Was that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, like a camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was like a camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a camp. They kept their prisoners before. So then when they took away the prisoners and let us live.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you paid for your work? Did you get any money for your work working in the forest?

speaker: interviewee
text: They supposed to give you money, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, how did you get food and how did you get clothing for your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to buy it? Did they give it to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we sold what we had. We sold and we bought food. We didn't have to buy it. They cooked one meal everyday. Supposed to buy it but nobody paid for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have in your one room where you lived, did you have a fireplace to keep warm?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- in your-- in your room with your family, did you have a fireplace to keep warm in the winter?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was forest, wood, a bunch of wood.

speaker: interviewer
text: But did you have a place to cook in your room?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: But one meal--

speaker: interviewee
text: Maria was cooking coffee. [LAUGHS] Maria was a home. And they want my wife to go out work. I says, no. My wife didn't go to work. I didn't let my wife go.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did the kids go to school there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the kids went to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of school was it for them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a Russian school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so the kids went to school. Now, how many people would you think were living there?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was about 200, Under about 200 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there guards there, or could you leave if you wanted to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, you couldn't leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, you could go to the village 15, 20 miles and buy something. We used to take some clothes to the village and get some potatoes, milk, or something else. As a matter of fact, the commandant had a cow there, just one cow. Nighttime, I'd pull the milk out.

speaker: interviewer
text: You milked the cow? And you lived there from May 1940, what were the winters like in Siberia? What were the winters like in Siberia? In the winter, during the winter when it was cold.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was 60 below, 70 below.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Stayed inside?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they gave us, what do you call it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Boots, like boots?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like boots, felt, what they gave us, yes. You couldn't go in shoes, not at all. If you go out with shoes you, you don't come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you work during the winter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, we worked. We made to a fire and sit around the fire. We didn't get much work, you know? I was working with a horse, pull the timbers out to a river, was in the river. They didn't get much work with us. And then when the war started, he says, you free. The same day, the same hour, it's a fact, I left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell about that. What did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went, it was to deliver, a deliver was going on a ship and pulling a load. It was pulling, how you call, a ship with that. We went in there, and we go to Tomsk.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of year was that? When was that?

speaker: interviewer
text: 1941? So from May 1940 until the war started in when?

speaker: interviewer
text: In '41. And what time of year was that? The summer or winter?

speaker: interviewee
text: We was there about 18 months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, did you observe any Jewish holidays there? Were you are able to have Pesach?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not special. If you want to. You do what you want. They don't stop you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you get matzo?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how about the Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur, were you able to celebrate those?

speaker: interviewee
text: If you want to stay home, you stay home. You don't have to go to work. Well, you might have to but if you don't go, you don't go.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't get punished then if you didn't go?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the people ever object or protest or anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they couldn't. Yeah, well, we made a strike once. We made a strike. We didn't go out to work for three days. Then they came from Moscow and talked to us. They're going be good. They're going to get better livings and so forth, and everybody went back. They didn't go too hard, but they says, if this would be Russian people doing they would be all shot. But Polish people they didn't bother too much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In that there.

speaker: interviewer
text: But why was it that way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this I don't know why.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they were Russian, who were in charge?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we was not citizens there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the Russians in charge?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Jewish people have any kind of government or any kind of--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Well, I received packages. I don't know until now who sent them to me from Israel. Two packages I received with everything in the packages, 15-pound packages. And I don't know who sent it to me until now. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have relatives or maybe the Joint?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had relatives, sure, but they didn't send. When I was in Israel and talked to the relatives, they says they don't know. But I suppose my brother was alive. Maybe he paid in and they sent it. But I didn't saw my brother any more.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your brother know what happened to you or did you know what happened to your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: When he was back, he wrote me a letter that he's happy when he went back. But the end was they took him to crematorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: He wrote you in what year was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Him with the whole family because he was with the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was it that you last heard from him?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a matter of fact, when I came back I find a cousin, a woman. And she lost her leg. She was standing-- by the river was eight-story buildings, two of them. She had a guy working for her with her husband. And that guy when he was working lived there on that eight-story building.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he had that woman between two walls stand for three years. In the nighttime she went down, worked a little and got back there. And she was, they was shot. And it hit her leg, took off her leg. So I met the woman. She was my cousin, and she says she had a daughter and a son, they went to Russia. And they-- rather they coming back when I came. They didn't came when I was there, So she was waiting for them. She had in San Francisco brothers, two. She says she's going to San Francisco. Her name was [NON-ENGLISH]. I remember. And she was there and waiting until the son and the son was here. The son was, picture make, like-- Photographer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not a photographer. Handwriting, he made pictures worth millions.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is it like an artist?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So it was wait-- and I didn't see her any more because we left to a different town.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she told you about your relatives, is that what you said?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a relative, yeah. But she had brothers San Francisco. She says she go to San Francisco.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw her after the war. Let's go back to when you were freed. You were freed out of Russia, out of Siberia. And you said you went to the river. Tell about what happened after that. Let's go back to that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I went back from Siberia?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I started working on a truck.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go after? You said you went down to the river.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the warm places, Tashkent. It's not winter, it's always summer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who all was with you when you went down to the river after you left the camp? Your children and your wife. Did your sister-in-law?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was always with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Always with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Always with me. Never left nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then you went to Tashkent. Tell about living there, what that was like.

speaker: interviewee
text: I started working on a truck.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get the truck?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the government's truck not my truck. As a matter of fact, I had to make the truck.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had to make the truck?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to make the motor. I had to make the chassis. It took about two months until I made it. And then I make a living. I was transferring wheat. All that I did is transfer wheat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell about your family. What did they do when you were working, when you were transferring?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they, did your son--

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife didn't work never.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wife didn't work.

speaker: interviewee
text: Never. The kids were in school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this? How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1941, '42, '43, '44. When I came back, I think, in '45, I came back to Germany

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you when the war ended?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you when the war ended?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Tashkent. Exactly, I was in Tashkent. We lived in Shymkent but I was in Tashkent May 9, and they announced the war is over. Oh, there was the people dancing on the roof and whiskey all over.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then what did you decide to do or what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: To go home, to Warsaw. But we didn't go to Warsaw. We went to Germany, Liegnitz. And then from Liegnitz, we stayed not long, two months, I think. And then we went to Czechoslovakia to Austria and to Germany, Stuttgart. In Stuttgart, I was living until I went to the States 1945, 1950.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, let's go back. So when you left, when you left in 19-- when the war was over and you went-- tell about going back to, you said you went back to Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I wasn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Warsaw.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't going any more to Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: I thought you went back to Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was once, with my wife, one day and that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't go back after the war? And then where-- What were your kids doing when you were staying in Czechoslovakia? You said you were Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewee
text: We just passed Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't stay, we passed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you went to-- where did you go next?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And to Austria, did you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Through [NON-ENGLISH], no, not to [NON-ENGLISH]. Salzburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was your family all together then, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, then I started picking up the family from here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me where the family was that you went to pick them up.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Dave and Maria are supposed to go to Israel. So they went to our camp and wait now to go to Israel. And we was, then when we left--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you and your wife then?

speaker: interviewee
text: In [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Stuttgart? No, Liegnitz? Liegnitz. Liegnitz. And when we left and the kids was in a camp. Then when I left, we go to Germany. We went in Salzburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you picked up the kids, you said?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then in Salzburg, we decide to pick up the kids not to let them go nowhere. So I went looking for the kids. And I go in one town-- I forgot the name-- I go on the street and I see Dave walking. And then somebody told me they saw Maria.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I got Dave, and we go look for Maria. There was a crematorium there by the forest at the end. And I go out and holler, Maria, Maria! And she heard me. [LAUGHS] She came out.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were sleeping in a-- it was a crematorium. There was a lot of boys and girls there. So I got Maria, too. And a lot of boys and girls-- were their parents was with me in-- in Salzburg. I went to all of them and went to Salzburg and brought them.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK we're going to end this tape again and start again in a minute.
